entry.title: Tapping into your organization's resourcefulness with SAP Funds Management
entry.content:
Introduction

Organizations often grow organically over time while taking on new mandates, expanding valuable services, and establishing new capabilities. With organic growth complexities naturally develop in how the organization is structured, managed, and funded. This presents leaders with an opportunity to take stock of their existing funds management model to unlock efficiencies.

For organizations using, or considering, SAP to manage their finances there are enormous opportunities to maximize the impact of their available funds. SAP Funds Management, specifically created for public sector clients, enables key functionality to help you structure your organization's funding model and the related real-time controls.

Funding Silos

Even with an assertive review process in place organizations may develop funding silos as they grow and reshape themselves over time. A funding silo can result in under-utilizing budgets in a timely manner and even encourage a 'use it or lose it' approach to budget management. 

Funding silos reduce flexibility by preventing money from being redirected to emerging pressures, even when surpluses exist elsewhere. They often lead to inefficient outcomes, such as lapses in one area alongside overspending in another, and encourage behavior aimed at protecting or exhausting budgets rather than improving results. Silos also increase administrative burden by requiring separate planning, forecasting, and controls, weaken enterprise‑wide decision‑making by reinforcing program‑centric thinking, and complicate explanations to executives and central authorities when financial pressures appear inconsistent with available funding.

Standard SAP features like Cover Groups, where one area's funding is permitted to cover another area's funding requirements, and Revenue Increasing  Budget (RIB) rules, where revenue generated from one source within your organization is eligible to be spent in another, allow you to avoid funding silos through thoughtful design. 

Cover Groups 

In SAP Funds Management, a cover group is a configuration mechanism that allows budget flexibility by enabling budget overruns in one budget address to be covered by available budget from another budget address within the same group. Instead of enforcing availability control (budget checks) strictly at each individual budget address, SAP can check the net available budget across the cover group, which helps accommodate legitimate spending patterns without re‑budgeting while still preserving overall budget discipline. SAP provides flexibility within its standard configuration for organizations to define which budget addresses can share budget as senders, receivers, or both. 

Cover group configuration is available to clients utilizing SAP Funds Management today and can be reliably implemented with minimal technical complexity. The process to setup cover group eligibility is to activate it for your Financial Management Area, setup a number range for your cover group(s), then define derivation rules to set conditions for each cover group you want to generate. Transaction FMCEGENCG can then be used to mass generate your cover eligibility rules. Once generated, your availability control (budget checks) take your groups into consideration while assessing whether availability control warnings or hard errors should be triggered. 

Note: You can also modify individual cover groups through transaction FMCERULE - Process Single CE Rule where you can change budget address roles to sender, receiver, or sender & receiver.

The most important aspect of cover group implementation is taking the time to understand and define the organization's financial management requirements correctly, including any rules and regulations surrounding budget utilization. 

Revenue Increasing Budget (RIB)

In addition to cover groups, SAP Funds Management can also leverage incoming revenue to increase budgets with tremendous flexibility made possible through its RIB (revenue increasing budget) functionality. Tying funding directly to realized revenue can help organizations streamline budgeting processes while adopting outcome based funding models for re-spendable revenue. Organizations have the ability to configure which budget addresses are available for RIB processes by establishing them as RIB objects. As an example, you may have a budget address containing a commitment with item category 02 (Revenues) identified as a source RIB object to feed another budget address containing a commitment with item category 03 (Expenditures).   

Once RIB objects have been designated through your RIB object derivation strategy you can setup your RIB calculation formulas, which allow you to configure the conditions related to re-spendable revenue such as whether or not all revenue collected is eligible to increase a budget or only a certain percentage of it. 

After the RIB object derivation is configured you can then configure your RIB rule definitions and derivations. The RIB rule derivation strategy will be triggered when you mass generate your RIB rules through transaction FMRBGENMD.  Similar to cover groups, you can also handle this manually or edit a single rule through transaction FMRBRULE.  All active RIB rules can then be triggered when you use transaction FMMPRBB, either ad-hoc or periodically depending on your organization's requirements.

Important: After RIB configuration changes you may need to re-initialize via transaction FMRBREINIT and  re-index via FMRBIDXREC.

For visibility into RIB activity you can leverage transaction FMRBMON01 to provide you with an overview of RIB values where you can see the results of the budget increases, giving you the traceability to monitor re-allocated funds within your organization. 

The commitment items 0003 and 0004 the image below make up part of the budget address associated to the RIB Objects. We can see that some of the revenue from 0003 has been transferred to 0004, thereby increasing the budget for the budget address associated with 0004 in this case. $500 remains available to transfer which can be picked up the next time the RIB rules are executed with transaction FMMPRBB.

Revenue and Expenditure Commitment Item Types

Conclusion

For organizations contemplating how best to manage their existing funding and streamline funds management processes these two features, cover groups and Revenue Increasing Budget, present organizations with robust out of the box capabilities. While the product capabilities come standard, the implementation complexities often come from difficulties obtaining the organization's full set of business requirements, edge case scenarios, policy restrictions, and change management implications. As with most financial modernization, a methodical and structured approach can help you improve your long established processes with confidence. The high-level steps below can serve as a starting point when planning out how to tap into these enhanced budgeting features. 

  1. Establish constraints and objectives by confirming funding boundaries, policy limits, risk tolerance, and where current funding silos create recurring pressures or lapses.

  2. Map the as‑is funding silo structure by documenting how funds, fund types,  and AVC rules segment budget, and identifying patterns of surplus and deficit.

  3. Test flexibility opportunities by assessing where SAP cover groups could safely pool fund availability across interchangeable budget lines, and where RIB could enable re-spendable revenue possibilities.

  4. Design and validate options by defining guardrails, modelling alternative configurations, and validating impacts with finance, program, and audit stakeholders.

  5. Pilot and institutionalize by trying selected cover group and RIB designs, measuring results, and embedding the approach into budgeting governance and SAP configuration standards. Once you've found something that works for your organization, explore scaling it and model out your return on investment in the form of reduced administration, efficient budget consumption, and more. 

If optimizing your organization's budget management strategy interests you, please feel free to reach out to me anytime for a chat.

Ben Kane

entry.title: Connect Claude AI to SAP Datasphere Using MCP: An Implementation Guide
entry.content:
Introduction:

If you've ever spent time in datasphere searching for objects in SAP Datasphere opening space after space, searching through views, models, tables etc this post is for you.

I wanted a better way. Instead of navigating the UI manually every time, I wanted to just ask my AI assistant: "What tables are in the SALES_SPACE?" or "Show me the first 10 rows of MONTHLY_ACTUALS" — and get an answer instantly. So I built an MCP server that connects Claude directly to SAP Datasphere's REST and OData APIs.

This post walks through what MCP is, how the integration works, what you can actually do with it today, and  importantly where its current limits are.

And here's why this matters now:  As of SAP Sapphire 2026, this isn't just a community experiment anymore — SAP and Anthropic announced a formal partnership to make Claude and MCP central to SAP's AI strategy.

What is MCP server?

An MCP server is a specialized program or service that follows the Model Context Protocol (MCP) — an open standard (introduced by Anthropic in late 2024) for connecting AI models (like large language models or LLMs) to external tools (like datasphere in this case), data sources, and systems in a secure, standardized way. More info here . 

An MCP server is a lightweight service that exposes a set of callable tools — functions the AI can invoke when it determines they're relevant to a user's question. The AI assistant discovers available tools automatically, decides when to use them, and handles the back-and-forth with the server transparently.

How it works?

The integration has three layers: your AI assistant (Claude), an MCP server running in Python, and SAP Datasphere's APIs. Here's how they connect:

The flow works like this: you type a natural language question to Claude. Claude recognizes it needs information from Datasphere, selects the appropriate MCP tool, and calls the MCP server. The server authenticates with Datasphere, makes the REST or OData API call, and returns the result. Claude formats the response and presents it to you.

You never see the API call. You just get the answer.

Building the MCP Server:

The MCP server is the bridge between your AI assistant and SAP Datasphere. It's built in Python using the FastMCP library, which handles all the MCP protocol scaffolding so you can focus on the actual API logic.

The core pattern is simple: every Python function decorated with McP.tool() is automatically registered as a discoverable, callable tool. When Claude sees a user question that requires Datasphere data, it identifies the relevant tool, calls it with the right parameters, and incorporates the result into its response.

Here's a minimal example showing how a metadata tool is defined:

 

from fastmcp import FastMCP

import requests

 

mcp = FastMCP('datasphere-mcp')

 

McP.tool() (@needs to be prefixed before)

def list_spaces(auth_token: str) -> dict:

    """List all available spaces in the Datasphere tenant."""

    url = f'{DATASPHERE_BASE_URL}/api/v1/dwc/catalog/spaces'

    headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {auth_token}'}

    response = requests.get(url, headers=headers)

    return response.json()

Every tool follows this same structure: define what it does in the docstring (Claude reads this to decide when to invoke it), make the API call, and return the result. FastMCP handles serialization and protocol communication automatically.

Step by Step Instructions: How to Setup MCP Server and connect to Claude

  1. Get your Datasphere session cookie — This is how your server authenticates with Datasphere. Open your browser's developer tools while logged into Datasphere, go to the Network tab, and copy the full cookie string from any request header.
  2. Create your project folder and install dependencies — Sets up your Python environment with the three libraries you need. fastmcp runs the MCP server, requests calls the Datasphere APIs, and python-dotenv loads your credentials safely.
  3. Create the .env file — Stores your Datasphere URL and cookie separately from your code so you never accidentally expose credentials. This is the only file you'll need to change between environments.
  4. Create server.py — This is the core of the integration. It defines all the tools Claude can call, each one mapping to a Datasphere API endpoint. I have provided the code here.
  5. Test the server runs — Before connecting Claude, confirm the server starts cleanly on its own. If it errors here, fix it before moving on.
  6. Install Claude Desktop — The desktop app is what connects to your MCP server locally. The web version of Claude doesn't support MCP servers yet.
  7. Register the server in claude_desktop_config.json — This tells Claude Desktop where your server lives. Without this step, Claude has no idea your server exists even if it's running.
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "datasphere": {
      "command": "C:\\Users\\username\\AppData\\Local\\Programs\\Python\\Python311\\python.exe",
      "args": [
        "C:\\Users\username\\Datasphere_MCP Agent\\server.py"
      ],
      "env": {
        "DATASPHERE_BASE_URL": "https://academydatasphere-1.ap11.hcs.cloud.sap",
        "DATASPHERE_COOKIES": ""
      }
    }
  },
  "preferences": {
    "coworkScheduledTasksEnabled": true,
    "ccdScheduledTasksEnabled": true,
    "coworkWebSearchEnabled": true,
    "sidebarMode": "chat"
  }
}

      8.  Restart Claude Desktop and verify — After restarting, click the tools icon in the chat input and            confirm datasphere appears in the list. That's how you know the connection worked.

      9. Ask your first question — You're live. Start with something simple like "What spaces do I have in my Datasphere tenant?" to confirm everything is working end to end.

Troubleshooting tip:

If Claude doesn't see the tools, check that server.py is still running, the config file path is correct for your OS, and your cookie hasn't expired.

Available Tools

The current implementation six tools, organized into two groups:

Category

Tool Name

What It Does

Metadata

list_spaces

Returns all available Datasphere spaces in the tenant

Metadata

list_tables

Lists tables, views, and analytical models within a space

Metadata

get_table_schema

Returns column names, data types, and nullability for any entity

Metadata

get_space_objects

Debug utility — dumps raw API response when assets aren't showing up as expected

Query

run_odata_query

Queries an entity via OData protocol with support for $filter and $select

Query

preview_table

Quick way to sample the first few rows of any table or view

Example conversations:

Finding tables in space:

 

Data of Graphical view and see below how it generates Key observations:

Limitations

  • Read-only operations only — no write-back, no model deployment, no space configuration changes.
  • Only objects with the Allow consumption flag enabled are accessible. Tables and views must be marked for consumption in the space settings, and analytical models must be deployed before they can be queried.
  • Cookie-based authentication is fragile for the trial setup — sessions expire frequently and require manual renewal. Not suitable for anything beyond personal demos.
  • Compared to our MCP server, the SAP CLI is significantly more powerful.

What's Next

I built this as a community experiment — and then SAP announced exactly this direction at Sapphire 2026, where they formally partnered with Anthropic to make Claude and MCP central to SAP's AI strategy. Sometimes the community gets there first. The immediate next step is switching to proper OAuth 2.0 client credentials, which will make the server stable and suitable for real use beyond trial environments.

Beyond that, the capability I'm most excited about is agentic data validation — giving an AI agent the ability to not just answer questions about Datasphere, but to proactively detect data quality issues and perform root cause analysis. Imagine an agent that notices revenue numbers look off, traces the issue back through dependent views, checks the underlying table for nulls or mismatches, and surfaces a diagnosis — all through the MCP tools, triggered by a single question. And then goes one step further: recommends how to fix the issue, waits for your approval, and executes the fix. That's the human-in-the-loop agentic workflow I want to build toward. 

Thank you!
Saravanan Chinnaswamy

entry.title: Re: After SO change, credit block triggered even within O...
entry.content:
Hello, 

the settings in OVA8 (days and percentage) are only relevant for manual released dcd's, the field Numbers of days without check specify the number of days after which a changed document must be rechecked.

The system does NOT carry out another credit check if the following conditions are met:

  • The value of the changed order is not greater than the value already approved for credit (inclusive of the deviation factor), AND
  • The current date is not greater than the original release date plus the number of days specified here.

In your case other relevant fields were changed, so the system triggered a new check and in the case that the result is negative the document will be blocked depending on the defined reaction in OVA8. 

In UKM_LOGS_DISLAY or the APP "Display Credit Management Log" you can see the result of the check. 

To answer your question, yes that is standard. 

Hope that helps?

kind regards

Stephan

entry.title: Securing SAP UI5 Applications with a Custom Node.js Backend using JWT
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Introduction

When I pair SAP UI5 with a custom Node.js backend, there's no BTP or XSUAA to provide security. JWT (JSON Web Token) is the right tool for providing security. It is stateless, lightweight, and works naturally with both Express middleware and the UI5 fetch API.

This guide covers the complete end-to-end setup:

  • MySQL — user table with bcrypt-hashed passwords and roles
  • Node.js backend — JWT generation on login, protected routes via middleware
  • SAP UI5 frontend — token storage, role-based routing, authenticated API calls, session expiry handling
  • Local dev proxy—ui5.yaml config so the UI5 dev server forwards /api calls to Node.js

 

Why I Wrote This

A few months back, I was working on a project where we had to build a lightweight internal tool on top of an SAP landscape — a UI5 frontend talking to a custom Node.js backend. The moment security came up in the design discussion, I realized there wasn't one clear, end-to-end guide covering this specific combination: SAP UI5 + Node.js + JWT + Role-Based Access Control.

This post walks through the complete flow: setting up the database, generating the JWT on login, storing it in the UI5 frontend, protecting your Node.js APIs with middleware, proxying API calls during local development, and handling session expiry gracefully.

 

First, Let's Understand JWT—The Wristband at the Door

Before jumping into code, here's how I explain JWT to anyone who's new to it.

Picture a nightclub with a VIP section. The first time you arrive, the bouncer checks your ID (your email and password). Once verified, they hand you a wristband — a JWT. For the rest of the night, you don't show your ID again. You just flash the wristband. The staff trusts it because the club itself issued it and it's tamperproof.

That's exactly what happens here:

  1. User submits their credentials to the Login API
  2. The backend verifies them against the database
  3. If valid, the server signs a JWT containing the user's ID, email, and role
  4. The UI5 app stores that token and attaches it to every subsequent request
  5. Each protected API checks the token before responding

Since the token is cryptographically signed with a secret key only the server knows, no one can fake or modify it.

 

Architecture Overview

The setup is a decoupled architecture—UI5 and Node.js live as completely separate projects and talk only through HTTP API calls. Here's the folder structure I used:

project-root/
│
├── backend/                        ← Node.js (Express) server
│   ├── .env                        ← Secret key and DB credentials (NEVER commit this)
│   ├── server.js                   ← Entry point — boots Express and registers routes
│   ├── repo/
│   │   └── dbconnection.js         ← MySQL connection setup
│   ├── middleware/
│   │   └── authMiddleware.js       ← JWT verification guard
│   ├── controller/
│   │   ├── userController.js       ← Login logic with bcrypt
│   │   └── adminController.js      ← Protected data APIs
│   └── routes/
│       ├── userRoutes.js           ← Public routes (login)
│       └── adminRoutes.js          ← Protected routes (require JWT)
│
└── frontend/                       ← SAP UI5 application
    ├── ui5.yaml                    ← Dev server config including API proxy
    ├── webapp/
    │   ├── controller/
    │   │   ├── View1.controller.js          ← Login page logic
    │   │   ├── AdminDashboard.controller.js
    │   │   └── UserDashboard.controller.js
    │   ├── view/
    │   │   ├── App.view.xml                 ← Root shell (contains App id="app")
    │   │   ├── View1.view.xml               ← Login screen
    │   │   ├── AdminDashboard.view.xml
    │   │   └── UserDashboard.view.xml
    │   └── manifest.json                    ← Router config lives here

 

Step 1: Setting Up MySQL

We need a database to store user credentials and roles. I'm using MySQL here. If you already have it installed, jump straight to the schema part.

Otherwise, install MySQL Community Server from the official MySQL site, along with MySQL Workbench for a visual interface.

Once you're logged into Workbench, open a new SQL tab and run this:

-- Create and select the database
CREATE DATABASE my_ui5_app;
USE my_ui5_app;

-- User table with role-based access
CREATE TABLE `user` (
  `user_id`        INT NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `user_name`      VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  `email`          VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  `contact_number` VARCHAR(15) DEFAULT NULL,
  `password`       VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,        
  `role`           VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'User',  
  PRIMARY KEY (`user_id`),
  UNIQUE KEY `email_UNIQUE` (`email`)
);

INSERT INTO `user` (`user_name`, `email`, `password`, `role`)
VALUES ('System Admin', 'admin@company.com', '$2b$10$cUSzmudHOeDXCF6eToP3buHZULwxuuJ/J5WZYeFkStg7HBpy2uCcW', 'Admin');

INSERT INTO `user` (`user_name`, `email`, `password`, `role`)
VALUES ('Shubham', 'shubham@gmail.com', '$2b$10$xBn6cKLkEuIkSqoLSR3pw.r16EABwE7aHtIDU4KSq2czl3LW5PMpK', 'User');

 

 

Step 2: Backend — Installing Dependencies and Generating Password Hashes

Let's get the backend ready first by using commands.

mkdir backend
cd backend
npm init -y
npm install express mysql2 jsonwebtoken bcrypt dotenv

Create a .env file in the backend root:

DB_HOST=localhost
DB_USER=root
DB_PASSWORD=your_mysql_password
DB_NAME=my_ui5_app
SECRET_KEY=your_very_long_random_secret_key_here
PORT=3306

Generating Real Password Hashes
Before inserting test users into MySQL, generate proper bcrypt hashes. Create generateHash.js in your backend folder—this is a one-time utility script, not part of the running app:

const bcrypt = require('bcrypt');

async function generateHashes() {
    const adminHash = await bcrypt.hash('Admin@123', 10);
    const userHash  = await bcrypt.hash('User@123', 10);

    console.log('Admin hash:', adminHash);
    console.log('User hash: ', userHash);
}

generateHashes();

 

Database Connection —repo/dbconnection.js

const mysql = require('mysql2');
require('dotenv').config();

const connection = mysql.createConnection({
    host:     process.env.DB_HOST,
    user:     process.env.DB_USER,
    password: process.env.DB_PASSWORD,
    database: process.env.DB_NAME
});

connection.connect((err) => {
    if (err) {
        console.error('DB connection failed:', err.message);
        return;
    }
    console.log('Connected to MySQL');
});

module.exports = connection;

 

Entry Point - server.js 
This boots the whole backend—wires together Express, registers the routes, and starts listening. I prefix all routes with /api to cleanly separate API traffic from any static file serving. 

const express = require('express');
const dotenv  = require('dotenv');

dotenv.config();

const userRoutes  = require('./routes/userRoutes');
const adminRoutes = require('./routes/adminRoutes');

const app  = express();
const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000;

// Parse incoming JSON request bodies
app.use(express.json());

// Register routes
app.use('/api/user',  userRoutes);  
app.use('/api/admin', adminRoutes);  

app.get('/api/health', (req, res) => {
    res.status(200).json({ status: 'Server is up and running' });
});

app.listen(PORT, () => {
    console.log(`Backend running at http://localhost:${PORT}`);
});

Start the server by giving commands to the terminal:
node server.js
npx nodemon server.js

 

Step 3: Backend — Login API with bcrypt and JWT

When the UI5 frontend submits credentials, the backend needs to:

  1. Find the user in the DB by email
  2. Compare the submitted password against the stored bcrypt hash using bcrypt.compare()
  3. If it matches, sign a JWT containing the user's ID, email, and role.

controller/userController.js:

const jwt    = require('jsonwebtoken');
const bcrypt = require('bcrypt');
const db     = require('../repo/dbconnection');

async function loginUser(req, res) {
    const { email, password } = req.body;

    if (!email || !password) {
        return res.status(400).json({ message: "Email and password are required." });
    }

    // Fetch user by email 
    const query = 'SELECT * FROM user WHERE email = ?';

    db.query(query, [email], async (err, results) => {
        if (err) {
            console.error('DB error:', err);
            return res.status(500).json({ message: "Internal server error." });
        }

        if (results.length === 0) {
            return res.status(401).json({ message: "Invalid email or password." });
        }

        const user = results[0];

        // bcrypt.compare() checks the submitted password against the stored hash
        const passwordMatch = await bcrypt.compare(password, user.password);
        if (!passwordMatch) {
            return res.status(401).json({ message: "Invalid email or password." });
        }

        // Build the JWT payload
        const tokenPayload = {
            user_id: user.user_id,
            email:   user.email,
            role:    user.role
        };

        // Sign the token using the secret key from .env — expires in 24 hours
        const token = jwt.sign(
            tokenPayload,
            process.env.SECRET_KEY,
            { expiresIn: '24h' }
        );

        return res.status(200).json({
            message: "Login successful",
            token:   token,
            user:    { role: user.role, name: user.user_name }
        });
    });
}

module.exports = { loginUser };

 

routes/userRoutes:

const express       = require('express');
const router        = express.Router();
const { loginUser } = require('../controller/userController');

router.post('/login', loginUser);

module.exports = router;

 

Step 4: Backend — The Middleware Guard 

This is the piece that protects every route requiring authentication. Every request to a secured endpoint passes through this function before reaching the controller.

middleware/authMiddleware.js:

const jwt = require('jsonwebtoken');

const verifyToken = (req, res, next) => {
    const authHeader = req.headers['authorization'];

    // Reject immediately if no Authorization header is present
    if (!authHeader || !authHeader.startsWith('Bearer ')) {
        return res.status(403).json({ message: "Access denied. No token provided." });
    }

    // Strip the "Bearer" prefix to get the raw token string
    const token = authHeader.split(' ')[1];

    jwt.verify(token, process.env.SECRET_KEY, (err, decoded) => {
        if (err) {
            // Token is expired or has been tampered with
            return res.status(401).json({ message: "Session expired or invalid token." });
        }

        req.user = decoded;
        next();
    });
};

module.exports = { verifyToken };

 

To protect a route, inject this middleware between the route path and the controller function:

routes/adminRoutes:

const express            = require('express');
const router             = express.Router();
const { verifyToken }    = require('../middleware/authMiddleware');
const { getItemDetails } = require('../controller/adminController');

// verifing Token 
router.get('/getItemDetails', verifyToken, getItemDetails);

module.exports = router;

 

controller/adminController.js:

const db = require('../repo/dbconnection');

function getItemDetails(req, res) {
    const query = 'SELECT * FROM user';

    db.query(query, (err, results) => {
        if (err) {
            console.error('DB error:', err);
            return res.status(500).json({ message: "Internal server error." });
        }

        return res.status(200).json(results);
    });
}

module.exports = { getItemDetails };

 

Step 5: Frontend—Configuring manifest.json

Open manifest. JSON. First, add sap.ui.layout to libraries inside manifest.json since I am using the SimpleForm control in the login view.

"libs": {
    "sap.m": {},
    "sap.ui.core": {},
    "sap.ui.layout": {}
}

Then replace the entire routing section with this:

"routing": {
    "config": {
        "routerClass": "sap.m.routing.Router",
        "controlAggregation": "pages",
        "controlId": "app",
        "transition": "slide",
        "type": "View",
        "viewType": "XML",
        "path": "ui5nodeapp.view",
        "async": true,
        "viewPath": "ui5nodeapp.view"
    },
    "routes": [
        {
            "name": "RouteLogin",
            "pattern": "",
            "target": ["TargetLogin"]
        },
        {
            "name": "RouteAdminDashboard",
            "pattern": "admin",
            "target": ["TargetAdminDashboard"]
        },
        {
            "name": "RouteUserDashboard",
            "pattern": "user",
            "target": ["TargetUserDashboard"]
        }
    ],
    "targets": {
        "TargetLogin": {
            "id": "View1",
            "name": "View1",
            "viewLevel": 1
        },
        "TargetAdminDashboard": {
            "id": "AdminDashboard",
            "name": "AdminDashboard",
            "viewLevel": 2
        },
        "TargetUserDashboard": {
            "id": "UserDashboard",
            "name": "UserDashboard",
            "viewLevel": 2
        }
    }
}

 

Step 6: Frontend — Proxying API Calls in Development

The UI5 dev server runs on port 8080 by default, while the Node.js backend runs on port 3000. So, I added the backend port to 3000 in the ui5.yaml file.

server:
  customMiddleware:
    - name: fiori-tools-proxy
      afterMiddleware: compression
      configuration:
        ignoreCertErrors: false
        backend:
          - path: /api
            url: http://localhost:3000   # Forward all /api calls to the Node.js backend
        ui5:
          path:
            - /resources
            - /test-resources
          url: https://ui5.sap.com
    - name: fiori-tools-appreload
      afterMiddleware: compression
      configuration:
        port: 35729
        path: webapp
        delay: 300
    - name: fiori-tools-preview
      afterMiddleware: fiori-tools-appreload
      configuration:
        flp:
          theme: sap_horizon

 

Step 7: Frontend — The Login View (XML)

view/View1.view.xml:

<mvc:View
    controllerName="ui5nodeapp.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:f="sap.ui.layout.form"
    displayBlock="true">

    <Page title="Login" showHeader="false">
        <content>

            <VBox
                justifyContent="Center"
                alignItems="Center"
                height="100%"
                class="sapUiMediumMarginTop">

                <Panel
                    width="380px"
                    class="sapUiResponsivePadding sapUiMediumPadding">

                    <VBox alignItems="Center" class="sapUiSmallMarginBottom">
                        <Title
                            text="Welcome Back"
                            level="H2"
                            class="sapUiSmallMarginBottom"/>
                        <Label text="Sign in to continue"/>
                    </VBox>

                    <f:SimpleForm
                        editable="true"
                        layout="ResponsiveGridLayout"
                        labelSpanL="12"
                        labelSpanM="12"
                        emptySpanL="0"
                        emptySpanM="0"
                        columnsL="1"
                        columnsM="1">

                        <f:content>

                            <Label text="Email Address" required="true"/>
                            <Input
                                id="loginUserId"
                                placeholder="Enter your email"
                                type="Email"
                                width="100%"
                                submit=".onLoggedIn"/>

                            <Label text="Password" required="true"/>
                            <Input
                                id="loginPassword"
                                placeholder="Enter your password"
                                type="Password"
                                width="100%"
                                submit=".onLoggedIn"/>

                        </f:content>
                    </f:SimpleForm>

                    <VBox alignItems="Center" class="sapUiSmallMarginTop">
                        <Button
                            text="Login"
                            type="Emphasized"
                            width="100%"
                            press=".onLoggedIn"/>
                    </VBox>

                </Panel>
            </VBox>

        </content>
    </Page>

</mvc:View>

 

Step 8: Frontend — The Login Controller

The login controller sends credentials to the backend, stores the token, and routes the user based on their role.

controller/View1.controller.js:

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/m/MessageToast"
], function (Controller, MessageToast) {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("ui5nodeapp.controller.View1", {

        onInit: function () {
        },

        onLoggedIn: async function () {
            const sEmail    = this.byId("loginUserId").getValue().trim();
            const sPassword = this.byId("loginPassword").getValue();

            if (!sEmail || !sPassword) {
                MessageToast.show("Please enter both email and password.");
                return;
            }

            try {
                const response = await fetch("/api/user/login", {
                    method:  "POST",
                    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
                    body:    JSON.stringify({ email: sEmail, password: sPassword })
                });

                const result = await response.json();

                if (!response.ok) {
                    throw new Error(result.message || "Login failed.");
                }

                // sessionStorage clears token automatically when the tab is closed
                sessionStorage.setItem("myAppToken", result.token);
                sessionStorage.setItem("myAppUser",  JSON.stringify(result.user));

                // Route to the correct dashboard based on role
                const oRouter = this.getOwnerComponent().getRouter();
                if (result.user.role === "Admin") {
                    oRouter.navTo("RouteAdminDashboard");
                } else {
                    oRouter.navTo("RouteUserDashboard");
                }

            } catch (error) {
                MessageToast.show(error.message || "Something went wrong. Please try again.");
            }
        }
    });
});

 

After logging in, we can check the token by clicking "inspect," then "application," then "session storage," and here we can see the generated token. 

 

Step 9: Frontend — Making Secure API Calls from the Dashboard

Any time the dashboard needs protected data, it reads the token from sessionStorage and attaches it to the Authorization header. It also handles session expiry cleanly.

controller/Dashboard.controller.js:

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel",
    "sap/m/MessageToast"
], function (Controller, JSONModel, MessageToast) {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("ui5nodeapp.controller.Dashboard", {

        onInit: function () {
            this.loadSecureData();
        },

        loadSecureData: async function () {
            const token = sessionStorage.getItem("myAppToken");

            if (!token) {
                this._redirectToLogin("No active session. Please log in.");
                return;
            }

            try {
                const response = await fetch("/api/admin/getItemDetails", {
                    method:  "GET",
                    headers: {
                        "Accept":        "application/json",
                        "Authorization": "Bearer " + token
                    }
                });

                // 401 = token expired or tampered or missing token header
                if (response.status === 401 || response.status === 403) {
                    this._redirectToLogin("Your session has expired. Please log in again.");
                    return;
                }

                if (!response.ok) {
                    throw new Error("Failed to load data. Please try again.");
                }

                const data   = await response.json();
                const oModel = new JSONModel(data);
                this.getView().setModel(oModel, "secureData");

            } catch (error) {
                console.error("API error:", error);
                MessageToast.show(error.message);
            }
        },

        _redirectToLogin: function (message) {
            sessionStorage.removeItem("myAppToken");
            sessionStorage.removeItem("myAppUser");
            MessageToast.show(message);
            this.getOwnerComponent().getRouter().navTo("RouteLogin");
        },

        onLogout: function () {
            this._redirectToLogin("You have been logged out.");
        }
    });
});

 

A Few Things to Keep in Mind:

On sessionStorage vs localStorage: I chose sessionStorage because it clears automatically when the browser tab closes, reducing the exposure window on shared machines. Both are vulnerable to XSS attacks, so consider a content security policy header in production.

HTTPS is non-negotiable: JWTs in transit are meaningless without HTTPS. Local development over HTTP is fine, but before you deploy anywhere, put the app behind HTTPS.

Token expiry: The 24-hour expiry works for many internal tools. For anything more sensitive, tighten it to 1–2 hours and consider adding a refresh token mechanism.

Role checks on the backend too: The frontend role-based routing is purely a UX decision — it is not a security control. Always enforce role checks on the backend as well. 

 

Conclusion:

That's the complete picture—from MySQL setup and real bcrypt password hashing through JWT generation and middleware protection on the backend to the UI5 frontend handling token storage, role-based routing, and secure API calls.

entry.title: Deep Insert in SAP CAPM Using Composition and Fiori Elements
entry.content:
Introduction 

In enterprise applications, it is very common to create a parent record together with one or more child records in a single transaction. For example, while creating a department, users may also want to maintain all Employees belonging to that department at the same time. 

In SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAPM), this requirement is handled through Deep Insert. 

Deep Insert allows us to send a single OData request containing: 

  • Parent entity data  
  • Related child entity data  

CAP automatically processes the complete payload and inserts all records in one transaction. 

In this blog, we will implement Deep Insert using the following entities: 

  • Department as the parent entity  
  • Employee as the child entity  
  • Composition to define the parent-child relationship  
  • Service projections in srv/emp.cds  
  • Fiori Elements annotations in app/annotations.cds 

 

Business Scenario 

Assume we are developing an HR application. 

When a user creates a new Department, they should also be able to add multiple Employees within the same Create page. 

For example: 

Department Details 

  • Name: Information Technology  
  • Manager Name: Hari Kumar  
  • Location: Bangalore  

Employees 

  • John Doe  
  • Jane Smith  
  • David Miller  

When the user clicks Save, both the Department and all Employee records should be inserted automatically. 

 

Data Model Design 

The data model consists of two entities: 

  1. Department  
  1. Employee  

The Department entity contains a composition named employees, which establishes a strong parent-child relationship. 

This is the key requirement for Deep Insert. 

 

Db/datamodel.cds: 

Department entity:

namespace harikumar.db;

entity Department {
    key ID              : UUID;
    NAME                : String;
    description         : String;
    location            : String;
    managerName         : String;
    managerEmail        : String;
    budget              : Decimal(15,2);
    employeeCount       : Integer;
    establishedDate     : Date;
    departmentCode      : String(10);
    status              : String;
    phoneNumber         : String;
    costCenter          : String;
    floorNumber         : Integer;
    buildingName        : String;
    employees           : Composition of many Employee on employees.department = $self;
}

employees : Composition of many Employee on employees.department = $self; 

  • This statement is the most important part of the implementation. 

Meaning of This Statement 

  • Composition defines a parent-child relationship.  
  • many Employee means one department can have multiple employees.  
  • employees.department = $self links each employee back to the current department.  
  • Child records are created, updated, and deleted together with the parent. 

Why Composition is Important 

Deep Insert works automatically only when child entities are modeled using Composition. 

If you use only Association, CAP will not automatically create child records during the same request. 

Employee Entity: 

entity Employee {
    key ID              : UUID;
    nameFirst           : String;
    nameMiddle          : String;
    nameLast            : String;
    nameInitial         : String;
    gender              : String;
    validFrom           : Timestamp;
    validTo             : Date;
    language            : String;
    phoneNumber         : String;
    email               : String;
    loginName           : String;
    currency_code       : Integer;
    salaryAmount        : Integer;
    accountNumber       : String;
    bankId              : String;
    bankName            : String;
    status              : String;
    department          : Association to Department;
}

 

department : Association to Department; 

  • This association stores the foreign key reference to the parent department. 
  • Each employee belongs to exactly one department 

 

Service Layer Implementation (srv/emp.cds) 

The service layer exposes the database entities as OData services 

using {harikumar.db as db} from '../db/datamodel';
service CatalogService @(path:'/CatalogService') {
    @odata.draft.enabled:true
     entity departmentSet as projection on db.Department{
        *,
        employees
     };
    entity employeeSet as projection on db.Employee;  
}

@odata.draft.enabled : true 

Enables draft support for Fiori Elements. 

Benefits: 

  • Save as draft  
  • Resume editing later  
  • Transactional consistency 

 

entity departmentSet as projection on db.Department { 

*, 

employees 

}; 

Why employees Must Be Included 

This projection exposes the composition to the service. 

Without this line: 

  • The Employees section will not appear in the Object Page.  
  • Deep Insert payload will not contain child entities.  
  • CAP cannot process nested data. 

app/project1/annotations.cds:

using CatalogService as service from '../../srv/emp';
annotate service.departmentSet with @(
    UI.LineItem:[
        {
            $Type:'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Name',
            Value:NAME
        },
        {
            $Type:'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Manager Name',
            Value:managerName
        },
        {
            $Type:'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Manager Email',
            Value:managerEmail
        },
        {
            $Type:'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Phone Number',
            Value:phoneNumber
        },
        {
            $Type:'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Status',
            Value:status
        },
    ],
    UI.FieldGroup #DepartmentDetails : {
        $Type : 'UI.FieldGroupType',
        Data : [
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Name',
                Value : NAME
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Description',
                Value : description
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Location',
                Value : location
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Manager Name',
                Value : managerName
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Manager Email',
                Value : managerEmail
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Budget',
                Value : budget
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Employee Count',
                Value : employeeCount
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Establish Date',
                Value : establishedDate
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Department Code',
                Value : departmentCode
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Status',
                Value : status
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Phone Number',
                Value : phoneNumber
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Cost Center',
                Value : costCenter
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Floor Number',
                Value : floorNumber
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Building Name',
                Value : buildingName
            }
        ]
    },
    UI.Facets : [
        {
            $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
            ID : 'DepartmentFacet',
            Label : 'Department Information',
            Target : '@UI.FieldGroup#DepartmentDetails'
        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
            ID : 'EmployeeFacet',
            Label : 'Employee Information',
            Target : 'employees/@UI.LineItem'
        }
    ],
    UI.HeaderInfo:{
        $Type: 'UI.HeaderInfoType',
        TypeName: 'Purchase Order',
        TypeNamePlural : 'Purchase Orders',
        Title:{
            Label:'PO Id',
            Value: ID
        },
        Description:{
            Label: 'Description',
            Value: phoneNumber
        },
        ImageUrl:'https://encrypted-tbn0.gstatic.com/images?q=tbn:ANd9GcTXPZNd0nkIK521Ft-9K4dzOaurGo-2zsslSA&s'

    },
    UI.SelectionFields:[
        NAME
    ],

);

annotate service.employeeSet with @(
    UI.LineItem : [
        {
            $Type : 'UI.DataField',
            Label:'First name',
            Value : nameFirst
        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Middle Name',
            Value : nameMiddle
        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Last Name',
            Value : nameLast
        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Initial Name',
            Value : nameInitial
        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Gender',
            Value : gender
        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Email',
            Value : email
        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Phone Number',
            Value : phoneNumber
        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Language',
            Value : language
        }
    ]
);


annotate service.departmentSet with {
    NAME @Common.ValueList : {
        $Type : 'Common.ValueListType',
        CollectionPath : 'departmentSet',
        Parameters : [
            {
                $Type : 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut',
                LocalDataProperty : NAME,
                ValueListProperty : 'NAME'
            }
        ]
    }
};

 

How Deep Insert Works in the UI 

When the user creates a new Department: 

  1. Department fields are entered.  
  2. The Employee Information section appears as a child table.  
  3. Users click Create to add multiple employees.  
  4. Users fill employee details.  
  5. Clicking Save sends one nested payload.  
  6. CAP inserts both Department and Employees in a single transaction. 

First click on create button, it will enable input fields for create record in department

Then fill all the input fields 

And again click on create button to create records for employee table and click on save  

After clicking on save Department record and employee record is going to create 

OData Payload Generated During Save

 

Conclusion: 

Deep Insert in SAP Cloud Application Programming Model provides an efficient way to create parent and child records in a single OData request. By using Composition between Department and Employee entities, CAP automatically manages related data without additional custom logic. During implementation, I found this approach very useful because it reduced development complexity and minimized multiple API calls. It also ensured transactional consistency, where both parent and child records are created together successfully. The integration with SAP Fiori elements made the UI experience smooth and user-friendly. Users can create departments and multiple employees from a single Object Page, which simplified data entry. This approach also improved maintainability and reduced backend coding effort. Overall, Deep Insert is a powerful feature for building scalable enterprise applications in CAPM. Have you used Deep Insert in your projects, and what was your experience with it?

entry.title: From Blueprint to Reality: SAP BDC Data Architecture Miniseries -June 2026
entry.content:
The SAP Business Data Cloud Data Architecture Miniseries is a focused, four-episode live session program running from June 1-5, 2026, designed exclusively for SAP Partners.

Each session is 1 hour, delivered online, and packed with practical guidance, demos, and strategic insights. Whether you’re just getting started or looking to sharpen your technical edge, this miniseries has something for every experience level.

Episode 1 - June 1, 2026: Leveraging SAP Architecture Center for SAP Business Data Cloud

Kick off the series with an introduction to the SAP Architecture Center, a powerful resource hub now live and with SAP Business Data Cloud at its core.

In this opening session:

  • What the SAP Architecture Center offers — solution reference architectures, diagrams, scenarios, component lists, and use case examples
  • How SAP Partners can leverage it for top SAP BDC use cases, including Enterprise Data & Analytics Strategy, SAP BW Modernization, SAP Databricks integration, and AI for SAP BDC
  • How to integrate these resources into your Enterprise Architect toolchain (draw.io and more)
  • How SAP Partners can actively contribute to the Architecture Center

Episode 2 -June 2, 2026: Unpacking SAP Business Data Cloud for Data Architects

Dive deep into the technical and strategic architecture of SAP Business Data Cloud in this expert session tailored for solution architects.

Key topics covered:

  • Introduction to SAP BDC and its role in unifying SAP and non-SAP data — structured and unstructured — with built-in governance, analytics, and AI
  •  Data products as reusable, governed assets and a strategic approach to data management
  • Technical architecture highlights: zero-copy sharing, object store persistence, and SAP Databricks integration
  • Live demo of an SAP BDC-powered intelligent application
  • Overview of the Data Product Catalog, metadata management, and lifecycle features
  • How SAP Partners can add unique value and best practices

Episode 3 - June 3, 2026: Leading SAP BW Modernization as a Data Architect

SAP BW modernization is one of the most critical journeys for many organizations today. This session provides solution architects with clear pathways and practical guidance for modernizing SAP BW and integrating with SAP BDC.

Key topics include:

  • The strategic value of SAP BW modernization - simplified architecture, greater agility, and readiness for AI-driven use cases
  • A comprehensive overview of SAP BW Modernization pathways
  • Common use case patterns where SAP BW queries and models are transformed into agile SAP BDC data products, enabling advanced dashboards and AI/ML capabilities
  • Details on the Free SAP BW to SAP BDC Migration Assessment — a tailored migration road map for your landscape
  • How to add unique value and best practices as an SAP Partner

Episode 4 - June 5, 2026: Planning Your Transition Paths to SAP Business Data Cloud

Close out the miniseries with a strategic session focused on architecture options and transition planning for moving to SAP Business Data Cloud — enabling unified data operations, faster innovation cycles, and insight-driven decision-making.

Key topics include:

  •  Transition Scenario Benefits and value realization frameworks
  •  SAP HANA Cloud + SAP BDC — bi-directional data exchange to power hybrid innovation
  •  Greenfield or Migration — flexible paths for both fresh starts and phased modernization
  • Seamless connection of data products with SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Databricks, and more
  • SAP’s complimentary assessments and services to help you plan and move confidently
  • Strategic roadmap and practical steps for a smooth, successful transition

Who Should Attend?

This miniseries is designed for: Architects | Consultants | Presales | Project Managers | Data Scientists | Data Analysts | Administrators

🔗Register Now

Don’t miss this opportunity to build your SAP BDC architecture expertise alongside the broader SAP Partner community. Sessions are available in multiple timezones.

LSC Registration Link

Alternative link (PE)

entry.title: RAP Recommendations
entry.content:
Sometimes, filling out forms is quite cumbersome. Like when you need to select the country you live in from a dropdown and scroll through all the countries, while you know that the application could have just preselected the country. Going further, would it not be great to write a description of where you are traveling and have recommendations for the city automatically generated, such as accommodations or restaurants?

To achieve this, RAP recommendations are available with SAP BTP ABAP environment 2602 and SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2602. RAP recommendations are an input assistance that proposes field values for input fields in a RAP application on an SAP Fiori UI. The proposed field values are based on the user input and the interaction history.

Motivation

Before the introduction of RAP recommendations, business users had to manually enter input into input fields or select values from a value help. The problem with that is:

  • Data entries may differ slightly from user to user.
  • Thinking about each entry is not efficient.
  • Finding the right terms is challenging.

By providing RAP recommendations, these difficulties are improved:

  • Data entries are consistent.
  • Business users' efficiency is increased by consistency and the proposed values.
  • The most likely value is predicted from the data context.

RAP recommendations enrich value helps with context-aware suggestions. Currently, there are two approaches to implement RAP recommendations: generative AI and deterministic rules.

Implementation

Before implementing RAP recommendations in your application, we need to look at the prerequisites and steps.

Prerequisite

RAP recommendations are built on top of a value help. Therefore, you need to have a value help implemented for the same field where you want to implement the RAP recommendation.

Steps

  1. Define RAP recommendations in the behavior definition.
  2. Implement RAP recommendations in the behavior pool of your RAP business object (using generative AI or deterministic rules).
  3. Define RAP recommendations in the projection behavior definition.

To understand RAP recommendations in detail, you can download the ABAP Flight Reference Scenario (GitHub) for RAP recommendations on GitHub. There, RAP recommendations are used in a travel example: After typing a description, generative AI suggests a destination, and then another RAP recommendation is triggered, suggesting accommodations using deterministic rules. Below is this example in a shorter form.

Example

This example shows the implementation of RAP recommendations using generative AI and deterministic rules. As mentioned above, you can download this example on GitHub. The following screenshot shows the RAP application requesting travel information.

RAP Recommendations

In the example, the business user selected Bulgaria as the destination. Right after the selection, three (fictive) accommodation recommendations for Sofia appear, and the business user can select the most fitting entry. Now, we will get through the implementation details step by step.

Behavior Definition

Define the field for which you want to provide RAP recommendations:

field ( recommendations ) Destination, Accommodation;

Here, the two fields Destination and Accommodation are specified to show the recommended values on the UI.

Define the function that runs the behavior:

recommendation function GetRecommendedValuesFunction;

Hint: Only one function can be specified for the recommended values.

Define side effects to specify which field change triggers the recommendation function:

side effects
  {
    recommendation function GetRecommendedValuesFunction
      executed on field Description, field Destination;
  }

The input for the recommended values is based on the two fields Description and Destination.

Behavior Pool

Define how the recommended values are returned:

METHOD GetRecommendedValuesFunction.
    READ ENTITIES OF /dmo/r_traveltp_rec IN LOCAL MODE
       ENTITY Travel
       FIELDS ( Description Destination ) WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
       RESULT DATA(business_user_travel)
       FAILED failed.

    "...

    LOOP AT business_user_travel ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<travel>).
      "...

      INSERT VALUE #(  %tky = <travel>-%tky
                       %param-travel = VALUE #( destination = CORRESPONDING #( destination_rec_values )
                                                accommodation = CORRESPONDING #( accommodation_rec_values ) ) ) INTO TABLE result.
    ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

The fields Description and Destination of the Travel entity are read and returned in the business_user_travel internal table. Inside the LOOP statement, the transactional key %tky and the result parameter %param for the component travel are filled.

You can find a description of the complete example in Example: Implementing RAP Recommendations (SAP Help Portal).

Deterministic Rules

For the deterministic rules approach, you can, for example, simply implement a query with a condition against your business user data. For example:

SELECT DISTINCT acc_name
          FROM /dmo/acc_rec_h AS accommodation
          INNER JOIN _user_travel AS destination
          ON accommodation~acc_country = destination~Destination
        INTO TABLE @result.

In this query, the Destination value is compared with the generated acc_country value.

Generative AI

To use the generative AI approach, you need to set up a scenario using the ABAP AI SDK (SAP Help Portal). In your Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISLM) scenario, you can define prompt settings that are used to call the LLM Completion API. When the call is successful, the LLM's answer is returned. For example:

METHOD get_completion_ai.
    DATA(completion_api_instance) = get_completion_api_instance( ).

    IF completion_api_instance IS BOUND.
      TRY.
          DATA(completion_api_result) = completion_api_instance->execute_for_string( prompt_setting ).
        CATCH cx_aic_completion_api.
          RETURN result.
      ENDTRY.

      result = completion_api_result->get_completion( ).
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD get_completion_api_instance.
    TRY.
        result = cl_aic_islm_compl_api_factory=>get( )->create_instance( 'MyIntelligentScen' ).
      CATCH cx_aic_api_factory.
        RETURN result.
    ENDTRY.
  ENDMETHOD.

You can find a description of the complete example in Example: Implementing Generative AI Features for RAP Recommendations (SAP Help Portal).

Recommended Values

The recommended values expect a certain set of parameters:

INSERT VALUE #( value = <rec_values>
                score = 100 - counter
                suggested = COND #( WHEN counter = 1
                                    THEN abap_true
                                    ELSE abap_false ) ) INTO TABLE result.

value is a mandatory parameter and contains the recommended value. score and suggested are optional parameters. Using score, you can order the recommended values in the value help. Using suggested, you can enter a recommended value directly into the input field.

Projection Behavior Definition

To reuse RAP recommendations in the projection behavior definition, you need to add the following:

use recommendations;
use side effects;

Resources

  • Documentation on the overall concept (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model | SAP Help Portal)
  • Documentation on the implementation (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model | SAP Help Portal)
  • Syntax Documentation (ABAP Keyword Documentation | SAP Help Portal)

entry.title: Re: Custom field - Segment is not visible in BP screen
entry.content:
Hi,

unfortunately the attachment is not there. Usually this is a consulting question but I will give you a way how to solve this issue.

It seems that you have assigned the field group or view to a standard section which has been moved now. To avoid this I recommend to create your own section and assign custom view. After this you can assign your custom section at required screen.

At initial GUI screen run transaction BUPT and navigate to: SAP Menu ->Business Partner ->Control ->Screen Layout 

Best regards
Andi

entry.title: SAP Stammtisch Basel–Freiburg: Austausch trotz Ferienzeit und Eishockey-WM
Building a Lightweight Web Service with Node.js HTTP Module
entry.content:
The http module is a built-in library in Node.js that allows developers to create web servers and handle HTTP requests without using external frameworks like Express.js. It is lightweight, fast, and ideal for learning how web servers work internally

1. Understanding the Core Architecture

A basic Node.js web server works in three steps:

  1. Import the built-in http module.
  2. Create a server using http.createServer().
  3. Listen on a specific port using server.listen().

The server receives:

  • req → Incoming request from the client
  • res → Response sent back to the client

This architecture is event-driven and non-blocking, which makes Node.js highly efficient for handling multiple requests.

2. Basic Server Implementation

Create a file named server.js and add the following code:

How to Run the Application

Open the terminal and execute:

Now open the browser and visit:

You will see:

3. Handling Routes and HTTP Methods

Unlike frameworks such as Express.js, the native http module does not provide built-in routing. Developers must manually check the request URL and method.

Example:

4. Parsing Incoming Data (POST Requests)

Node.js processes incoming request data as streams. For POST requests, data arrives in chunks and must be collected manually.

Example:

Example POST Request Using cURL

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/submit \

-H "Content-Type: application/json" \

-d "{\"name\":\"Rama Krishna\"}"

5. Advantages of Using the Native http Module

  • No external dependencies required
  • Lightweight and fast
  • Better understanding of HTTP fundamentals
  • Suitable for microservices and APIs

Full control over request and response handling

6. Limitations

While the native http module is powerful, it requires more manual coding compared to frameworks like Express.js.

Common limitations include:

  • Manual routing
  • Manual body parsing
  • No middleware support
  • More boilerplate code

For enterprise-scale applications, frameworks such as Express.js or NestJS are often preferred.

Conclusion

The built-in http module in Node.js is an excellent choice for learning backend development fundamentals and building lightweight web services. It provides direct access to HTTP request and response handling without additional abstractions.

Understanding the native http module helps developers build a strong foundation before moving to advanced frameworks like Express.js.

entry.title: Next-Gen SAP Ariba Invoicing : SAP Managed — What, Why, and How
entry.content:
What is Next-Gen SAP Ariba Invoicing?

SAP Ariba Invoicing is SAP's cloud-based solution for centralizing and automating supplier invoice processing across your organization. It brings invoices from multiple backend systems — SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP S/4HANA on-premise — into a single unified view, so your accounts payable team works from one place instead of jumping between systems.

Next-gen SAP Ariba Invoicing is the latest generation of the product, built on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and delivered under the SAP Managed model. It goes beyond the classic version by adding:

  - Centralized invoice processing across all connected S/4HANA systems in one launchpad

  - AI-assisted invoice capture — upload PDF or XML invoices and let SAP Document Information Extraction automatically extract the fields

  - Built-in extensibility — add custom fields without custom development

  - Joule AI assistant — conversational AI to help users navigate and process invoices faster

  - Unified user management via SAP Cloud Identity Services — no BTP Cockpit needed for user onboarding 

  - Automated/Simplified Technical Integrations Simplified — auto-configured via Formation

  In short: Next-gen SAP Ariba Invoicing is not just an upgrade — it is a reimagined invoice management experience designed for simplicity, compliance, and scale.

Customer Managed vs. SAP Managed — What Changed?

  The biggest shift in next-gen SAP Ariba Invoicing is not just the features — it is who manages the infrastructure.

  Customer Managed (Classic) : In the classic model, your IT team was responsible for everything:

  - Running the SAP BTP Booster to set up the subaccount

  - Configuring entitlements, service instances, and destinations manually

  - Managing trust between your Identity Authentication Service (IAS) and BTP

  - Assigning role collections to users in BTP Cockpit

  - Handling technical integrations, certificate rotations and keeping up with SAP Integration updates

  This required deep BTP expertise, ongoing maintenance effort, and made it difficult to stay current with new SAP Ariba Invoicing capabilities.

  SAP Managed (Next-Gen)

  With next-gen SAP Ariba Invoicing, SAP takes over the infrastructure completely. Your team's responsibility shrinks to providing the business inputs — your S/4HANA system details, your IAS tenant, and your preferred data center. SAP handles everything else automatically.

 

 Customer Managed (Classic)SAP Managed (Next-Gen)
ProvisioningBTP Booster — customer runs itSAP for Me wizard — SAP manages it
BTP infrastructureCustomer's BTP Global AccountSAP's BTP Global Account
Infrastructure maintenanceCustomer's IT teamSAP
Updates & patchesCustomer-coordinatedSAP delivers automatically
User managementBTP Cockpit, manual role assignmentIAS user groups, auto-synced
Master data integrationManual configuration per systemSimplified — auto-configured via Formation
SAP Ariba LaunchpadNot availableIncluded
Administration CenterNot availableIncluded
Extensibility HubNot availableIncluded

What You Gain — Customer Benefits

Switching to the SAP Managed model is not just a provisioning convenience — it delivers real business outcomes for your organization.

  • Faster Time to Value : Your SAP Ariba Invoicing tenant is ready within the same business day. No lengthy BTP setup, no back-and-forth between IT and SAP — just complete the wizard and your team is processing invoices the same day.
  • Lower Total Cost of Ownership : SAP manages the BTP infrastructure, handles updates, and takes care of certificate rotations and scaling. Your IT team no longer needs BTP expertise to run and maintain the solution — reducing both operational cost and dependency on specialist skills.
  • Always Current : SAP delivers updates and new capabilities automatically. You are always on the latest version of SAP Ariba Invoicing without coordinating maintenance windows or managing upgrades yourself.
  • Unified Invoice Processing Across All Systems : Process invoices from multiple SAP S/4HANA systems — cloud and on-premise — in a single launchpad. Your accounts payable team works from one place, with one consistent experience, regardless of how many backend systems your organization runs.
  • Simplified User Onboarding : Assign a user to an IAS group and they are immediately active in SAP Ariba Invoicing — no BTP Cockpit access needed. User onboarding is fast, auditable, and manageable by your identity team without involving IT.
  • Extensible Without Custom Development : Add custom fields to invoices via the Extensibility Hub without writing code or opening a development project — changes take effect immediately.

How It Works (Non-Technical)

1. SAP for Me — "The Reception Desk"

This is the only place the customer touches. You go to me.sap.com, fill in a 6-step wizard (your S/4HANA system, your region, your identity provider), click Finish — and walk away.

Everything that happens after that is SAP's responsibility.

2. The SAP Provisioning Engine — "The Building Manager"

This is the black box. The customer never sees it, never configures it, never calls it directly.

When you click Finish in SAP for Me, this engine does everything automatically:

  • Sets up your tenant — your private SAP Ariba Invoicing environment in SAP's infrastructure
  • Connects your S/4HANA and wires up master data — not just the invoice flow, but also all the reference data your invoices depend on: supplier names, company codes, purchasing organizations, cost centers, tax codes, payment terms. The engine pulls this master data from your S/4HANA and keeps it synchronized in CIM automatically. When a new supplier is added in S/4HANA, CIM knows about it — no manual export/import, no data entry in two places
  • Keeps connections fresh — if SAP releases a new version or updates the integration format, the engine automatically resyncs the connections; you don't get a support ticket telling you to "resync your formation"
  • Creates your user access groups — sets up ready-made groups in your identity system ("Invoice Processor", "Administrator", etc.) so your HR/IT team can just assign people
You give SAP your system details; the engine handles all the plumbing — including keeping your master data in sync.

3. Formation — "The Bundle That Holds It All Together"

A Formation is simply the name for the connected package the engine creates.

Imagine ordering a home internet plan. The ISP doesn't just give you a cable — they activate your account, provision your router, connect it to the street cabinet, and verify the signal. The whole activated, working setup is what you receive.

A Formation is that for SAP Ariba Invoicing:

  • Your CIM tenant
  • Your S/4HANA system
  • Your identity/user management
  • All the connections between them grouped into one named bundle that SAP manages going forward

To represent it visually : 

Now lets see it in action.

Prerequisites — Prepare These Before You Start

The provisioning wizard cannot be saved and resumed, so have the following ready before you begin.

  1. Valid SAP Ariba Invoicing License :A valid license for SAP Ariba Invoicing must be visible in SAP for Me under Portfolio & Products.
  2. S-User ID with Cloud Service Permissions : Your SAP for Me user must have an S-User ID with permissions to request and manage cloud services. See Access and Authorizations for details.
  3. Foundation for SAP Business Suite Subscription : Ensure you have subscribed to Foundation for SAP Business Suite before provisioning. See the Foundation setup guide for instructions.
  4. SAP S/4HANA System Details : Have your S/4HANA hostname, client number, and communication user credentials ready — you will need these at Step 3 of the wizard.
  5. IAS Tenant URL : Your SAP Cloud Identity Services (Identity Authentication) tenant URL — this becomes the identity provider for all SAP Ariba Invoicing users.
  6. Data Center Preference : Decide your preferred region before starting — especially important if your organization has data residency requirements. Available options: EU30, EU11 (EU Access), AP30, US30.

 

How to Provision SAP Ariba Invoicing — Step by Step

Log in to SAP for Me and follow these steps.

Step 1 — Navigate to Your SAP Ariba Portfolio

Go to Portfolio & Products → My Product Packages. Locate your SAP Ariba Suite entitlement and click on it to open the solution overview dashboard.

 

Step 2 — Start Provisioning

Switch to the Applications tab. Locate the SAP Ariba Invoicing tile and click Start Provisioning → New Solution.

The six-step provisioning wizard opens.

Step 3 — General Information

Fill in:

  • Name — a descriptive name for your tenant (e.g. sap-ariba-invoicing-prod)
  • Path — your organizational provisioning hierarchy in SAP for Me
  • Business Type — choose Production, Test, or Development

 

Step 4 — Select Applications

SAP Ariba Invoicing is pre-selected. 

Step 5 — Set Up Systems

Connect your backend systems:

  • SAP S/4HANA — your ERP backend for invoice posting
  • Identity Authentication Service (IAS) — your identity provider for user authentication
  • Foundation system — required if your organization uses SAP's unified procurement suite

Once your IAS tenant is connected, SAP automatically sets up Identity Provisioning Service (IPS) job to sync user groups — no manual BTP configuration needed, only trigger configuration required

Step 6 — Configure Parameters

Select your Region — the BTP data center where your tenant will be hosted.

OptionLocation
EU30Germany, Frankfurt
AP30Australia, Sydney
US30US Central, Iowa
System DeterminedSAP selects the optimal data center 

Step 7 — Set Up Formations

SAP for Me auto-generates a formation name based on your solution name. Accept the default or customize it. The formation automatically handles all destination and connectivity configuration between SAP Ariba Invoicing and your S/4HANA system.

Step 8 — Review and Finish

Review your complete configuration. Once you click Finish, SAP's automated provisioning:

  • Creates a dedicated BTP consumer subaccount in SAP's Global Account
  • Subscribes to SAP Ariba Invoicing and all required platform services
  • Configures destinations for S/4HANA and IAS connectivity
  • Creates user groups in your IAS tenant and sets up automatic role sync
  • Registers your tenant URL in SAP for Me

Typical completion time: within next 30 mins to 2 hours the same business day.
